text: @wavetossed @BrianRoemmele Some liquids are conductors, some are insulators. Water, for example, is not very conductive unless it has dissolved ions. If you put a voltmeter across an acid the voltage would be 0, because it is conductive. Conductors allow potential differences to equalise.

raw.full_text: @wavetossed @BrianRoemmele Some liquids are conductors, some are insulators. Water, for example, is not very conductive unless it has dissolved ions. If you put a voltmeter across an acid the voltage would be 0, because it is conductive. Conductors allow potential differences to equalise.

text: @Timothy73112603 @BrianRoemmele Not quite. While there are more H+ ions in an acid, there are more negative ions to "cancel them out". Bulk liquids are always neutral. There are actually lots of charges in pure water because it splits into OH- and the hydronium ion, but it's still neutral overall.

raw.full_text: @Timothy73112603 @BrianRoemmele Not quite. While there are more H+ ions in an acid, there are more negative ions to "cancel them out". Bulk liquids are always neutral.
